review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for procedural questions about vectorization, symmetry-breaking initialization, and adaptive optimizers such as Adam. The steps are described directly, so no clip is needed.

question_bank.question: What is the recommended implementation habit when writing deep-learning code for speed?
choices: Prefer Python for-loops because they are easier to debug
Use vectorized tensor operations instead of explicit loops whenever possible
Write each neuron as a separate function call
Move all computation back to the CPU after preprocessing
question_bank.explanation: Vectorized tensor operations are much faster than explicit Python loops and map well to GPU-style hardware.

transcript_quotes: always use vectors matrices and tensors

question_bank.question: Why is initializing all weights to the same constant, such as zero, a bad idea?
choices: It makes the model too nonlinear
It creates symmetry so different features learn the same thing
It forces gradients to become larger for every layer
It prevents the use of matrix multiplication
question_bank.explanation: If all weights start identical, symmetric units receive identical updates and fail to diversify into useful different features.

question_bank.question: If you want a strong default optimizer to try first, which one does the segment recommend remembering?
question_bank.explanation: Adam is presented as the optimizer name to remember because it adapts update sizes per parameter and is less fragile than plain SGD.
